The CLI sync command (src/cli/commands/sync.ts) is stranded inside the integration adapter zone rather than the CLI zone, creating an undocumented tier-boundary crossing that is invisible to gateway enforcement tests — it should be pinned to the CLI zone.
src/analyze/ contains files from 3 zones (cli-mcp-interface, llm-analysis-engine, prd-proposal-pipeline) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries
File "src/cli/output.ts" has 89% of import neighbors in src/cli/commands/ but lives in src/cli/ — consider relocating

The five zones in this batch decompose into two structural categories: data islands (rex-runtime-state, project-configuration) with zero import edges and source zones (prd-proposal-pipeline, llm-analysis-engine, cli-mcp-interface) with dense bidirectional coupling. The data islands are architecturally healthy; all three source zones breach the cohesion floor simultaneously, indicating the zone decomposition does not reflect the package's actual module boundaries.

The prd-proposal-pipeline and llm-analysis-engine zones together form a single dense cluster (27+13 cross-imports) inside a single directory (src/analyze/) with no physical boundary enforcing their separation. Treating them as two zones adds complexity — two zone health reports, two sets of coupling warnings — without providing any architectural benefit. Merging them into a single analyze zone would eliminate 40 artificial cross-zone imports and raise average cohesion.

cli-mcp-interface is the structural weak point of the package: lowest cohesion (0.27), highest coupling (0.73), files spread across 9 directories, and imports from all other significant zones. It is the zone most likely to accumulate further coupling as new features are added. Splitting it along the MCP-server vs. batch-maintenance axis is the highest-leverage structural intervention available without reorganizing files.

The rex package has no zone that sits cleanly at the 'interface layer' between the CLI entry points and the domain core — cli-mcp-interface was apparently intended for this role but has been colonized by maintenance commands and MCP tooling. The absence of a dedicated, thin command-dispatch layer (one that imports from core and re-exports nothing) is why the core → cli bidirectional cycle formed: there was no stable interface layer for core to depend on that would not also create a cycle.

Cross-zone import counts reveal that 'core' (prd-domain-operations) is the sole gravity center of the package — it is imported by every other non-data zone. This makes core's 43-file size a single point of fragility: any API change in core requires coordinated updates across cli (31 imports), unit-cli (39 imports), validate (17), and store (18). The package lacks a stable, narrow public API layer between core internals and its consumers.

All three micro-zones (prd-repair-command, task-selection-engine, schema-storage-reporting) are test-adjacency artifacts: community detection grouped test files with their imported source files rather than finding production-code clusters — applying a blanket rule that test files inherit their source file's zone (or are pinned to a dedicated test zone) would eliminate all three artificial zones in one pass

The rex package's zone graph has two distinct structural problems that compound each other: (1) prd-domain-operations is over-large and absorbs 150+ inbound imports, making it a hub, and (2) three micro-zones exist only because tests fragment what should be part of prd-domain-operations — fixing both simultaneously (merge micro-zones into domain, then split domain into schema/mutations/sync layers) would produce a stable 3-to-4 zone architecture for the package core

The cli → unit-cli → store import chain (cli-mcp-interface → rex-cli-command-layer → integration-adapter-layer) reveals that the MCP server is reaching through two CLI-tier zones to access persistence — a direct MCP → store path through the domain gateway would be shorter, more testable, and would eliminate lateral CLI-tier coupling

The cross-zone import matrix shows a clean directional flow (CLI tiers → domain → tree primitives) is already present in the majority of imports; the architectural violations are concentrated in exactly two edges: core → cli (13 imports) and core → unit-cli (13 imports) — resolving these 26 imports would make the entire import graph acyclic

The three zones with critically low cohesion (task-selection-engine: 0.17, schema-storage-reporting: 0.17, prd-repair-command: 0.25) collectively own only 18 files but generate disproportionate architectural noise; dissolving all three into their natural parent zones (prd-domain-operations and rex-cli-command-layer) would raise the package average cohesion while reducing zone count from 10+ to a more manageable set

The cross-zone import table shows two production zones (core, cli) importing from zones whose names carry the unit prefix (unit-cli: 13 from core, unit-analyze: 26 from cli) — if these zones contain production code the naming convention is actively obscuring tier boundaries and may cause gateway enforcement tests to miss real violations.

The unit-analyze and unit zones exhibit bidirectional coupling (unit-analyze→unit: 27, unit→unit-analyze: 13) while also receiving 26 imports from cli — this three-node sub-graph (cli, unit, unit-analyze) forms a semi-isolated circulation cluster within the analyze subsystem that is structurally separate from the core PRD domain graph; it is a candidate for extraction into a dedicated analyze sub-module.

Only two zones achieve cohesion 1.0 and coupling 0.0 (notion-integration-client and rex-e2e-test-suite); both share the pattern of a single production file (or zero production files) paired with co-located tests and no outbound runtime imports — this demonstrates that the Louvain algorithm produces accurate results when file scope is tightly constrained, validating the hypothesis that the low-cohesion zones suffer from artificial scope inflation rather than algorithm failure.

The five zones in this batch divide cleanly into two structural archetypes: stable islands (notion-integration-client, rex-e2e-test-suite — both cohesion 1.0) and fragmented satellites (tree-dag-primitives, prd-structural-validation, integration-adapter-layer — all cohesion ≤ 0.38); the common factor in the fragmented group is that each bundles files from more than one architectural tier (CLI + domain, or domain + tests), confirming that tier-mixing is the primary driver of low cohesion in this package.

All three source zones in this batch (prd-proposal-pipeline: 0.37, llm-analysis-engine: 0.38, cli-mcp-interface: 0.27) breach the 0.4 cohesion floor simultaneously. This is not a coincidence — it indicates the Louvain community detection is drawing boundaries that cut across natural module clusters. The underlying import graph has stronger affinity within src/analyze/ and within src/cli/ than the zone assignments reflect.

The two data zones (rex-runtime-state, project-configuration) have cohesion 0 because they contain no import edges — but they are reported alongside source zones in health dashboards using the same thresholds. Zone health tooling should exclude data/config zones from cohesion and coupling threshold checks to prevent them from skewing package-level health scores.

Test files are the primary driver of low-cohesion micro-zones in the rex package; a zone detection policy that excludes test files from community detection (or pins them to their source file's zone) would eliminate schema-storage-reporting, task-selection-engine, and prd-repair-command as artificial zones

The rex package exhibits a hub-and-spoke topology centered on prd-domain-operations rather than a layered architecture — all five analyzed zones import from the hub but the hub also imports back into two of them, producing a wheel-with-crossed-spokes pattern that is characteristic of a domain layer that accumulated CLI utilities over time

The analyze sub-graph (cli→unit-analyze: 26, unit-analyze→unit: 27, unit→unit-analyze: 13, unit→core: 24) forms a tightly coupled internal circulation loop that is structurally decoupled from the main PRD domain operations hub; these four zones collectively behave as a bounded sub-system and would benefit from explicit boundary documentation or consolidation into a single analyze zone.

Zone naming uses the unit prefix for zones that receive production-layer imports (unit-cli receives 15 imports from cli; unit-analyze receives 26 from cli) — the prefix implies test-only scope but the import evidence suggests mixed production/test content; standardizing zone names to reflect actual content (e.g., cli-commands instead of unit-cli) would eliminate ambiguity in tier-boundary enforcement.

The dependency graph for the five source zones in this batch forms a directed path: cli-mcp-interface → llm-analysis-engine → prd-proposal-pipeline → core, but with back-edges at every step. None of the three pairwise relationships is unidirectional. A clean layered architecture would require eliminating the back-edges first (especially core → cli: 13 imports) before any zone-split or merge will produce stable cohesion improvements.

The two critical architectural violations in the rex package are both reverse-direction imports from prd-domain-operations back into CLI-tier zones (core → cli: 13, core → unit-cli: 13); these 26 imports are the sole cause of circular zone coupling and their resolution is a prerequisite for any meaningful cohesion improvement

core imports from unit-cli (13 imports) — a production domain zone importing from a CLI-test zone is a tier inversion more severe than the already-noted core→cli cycle; domain code must not depend on test infrastructure regardless of whether that infrastructure is labeled unit or integration.

rex-cli-command-layer (unit-cli) independently imports from tree-dag-primitives with 11 imports, parallel to prd-domain-operations' own 20 imports — two distinct zones access tree primitives directly without going through the domain layer; any merge of tree-dag-primitives into prd-domain-operations must redirect 11 CLI-tier imports to avoid silently creating a new undeclared cross-zone dependency

prd-domain-operations has reverse imports into both cli-mcp-interface (13) and rex-cli-command-layer (13) simultaneously — the domain layer is coupled to two independent CLI surfaces, meaning a refactor of either CLI zone independently propagates into domain compilation; resolving only one of the two reverse edges leaves the domain still broken and the architectural violation still present

The single import from prd-domain-operations into prd-structural-validation (the '1' in the 1+17 bidirectional coupling) is a solitary reverse dependency from the domain into the validation layer — a targeted extraction of this one symbol into a shared constants or schema module would sever the only core→validate reverse edge cleanly without requiring a broader refactor of either zone

The analyze sub-graph (cli-mcp-interface, llm-analysis-engine, prd-proposal-pipeline) contains no unidirectional edge pairs — every pair of zones has imports flowing in both directions, making this a fully cyclic three-node cluster with no acyclic subset to use as a starting point for incremental untangling; unlike the domain-layer violations (core→cli, core→unit-cli) which are point violations in an otherwise DAG-structured graph, the analyze sub-graph requires a coordinated multi-zone restructuring to resolve

The 'core → cli: 13 imports' and 'core → unit-cli: 13 imports' entries in the cross-zone table conflate two distinct root causes: (1) zone misclassification of add.ts and reorganize.ts into prd-domain-operations, and (2) a single genuine tier inversion in core/move.ts. Treating these 26 imports as uniformly requiring refactoring is incorrect — the misclassification artifacts should be resolved first via zone pinning, leaving only the CLIError relocation as an actual code fix

The only file in src/core/ that imports from src/cli/ is core/move.ts, and only for CLIError. The previously reported 13+13 bidirectional coupling count is not 26 files of domain→CLI code — it is dominated by misclassified CLI commands appearing in the domain zone's membership, making the coupling metric misleading as an indicator of true architectural debt

Pinning add.ts and reorganize.ts to rex-cli-command-layer and relocating CLIError to src/errors.ts are together sufficient to make the rex package import graph fully acyclic — these two targeted changes resolve the highest-severity architectural issue without any algorithmic or business-logic modifications

The rex package has no integration test zone — 5 integration test files in tests/integration/ are distributed across multiple zones by co-location with their imported source files, making it impossible to measure integration test coverage as a zone-level metric the way e2e coverage is measured in rex-e2e-test-suite

epic-correlation.ts (in prd-structural-validation) imports from src/analyze/dedupe.js (in llm-analysis-engine/prd-proposal-pipeline zone) — this creates a cross-subsystem dependency from the validation zone into the analyze engine that is not visible in the direct zone-coupling table, meaning the true coupling surface of prd-structural-validation is larger than reported

The two zones with cohesion 1.0 (notion-integration-client, rex-e2e-test-suite) achieve it through physical directory discipline: one directory, one purpose. Every zone below 0.4 cohesion spans 5+ directories. Directory co-location is the strongest predictor of zone cohesion in this package — structural fixes that do not consolidate files into coherent directories will not improve cohesion scores even after zone re-analysis

The integration-adapter-layer is the only zone whose files span both a CLI-tier directory (src/cli/commands/) and a domain-tier directory (src/store/) simultaneously. Every other cross-tier issue in this package involves a single misplaced file; this zone has a structural composition that requires splitting rather than pinning

The store-contract.test.ts in notion-integration-client defines the behavioral contract for all adapters (Jira, Notion, Linear) but is anchored to a vendor-specific zone — it is a cross-cutting governance artifact masquerading as a unit test, and its current placement means future adapters must either duplicate the contract test or import across a zone boundary to reuse it

prd-domain-operations imports from both cli-mcp-interface (13) and rex-cli-command-layer (13) as two independent reverse dependencies into the CLI tier — if either CLI zone is refactored separately the domain compilation breaks; the two violations must be addressed together to achieve a clean domain boundary, and the shared symbols driving these 26 imports should be extracted into a foundation module

rex-cli-command-layer directly imports tree-dag-primitives (11 imports) in parallel with prd-domain-operations' own 20 imports — the CLI layer bypasses domain abstraction for tree operations, creating two independent access paths to the same primitives and preventing prd-domain-operations from owning or encapsulating tree utility behaviour

The analyze sub-graph (cli-mcp-interface ↔ llm-analysis-engine ↔ prd-proposal-pipeline) has bidirectional imports across all three zone pairs with no unidirectional edges — it forms a fully connected directed cycle cluster that cannot be partially resolved one edge at a time; a coordinated intervention (extract shared types, define a single pipeline entry point, eliminate reverse imports into cli-mcp-interface) is required to break all cycles simultaneously

The cross-zone import metric 'core → unit-cli: 13' is an aggregate that mixes zone-misclassification artifacts (CLI commands appearing in the domain zone) with a genuine tier inversion (core/move.ts importing CLIError). Using this metric directly to prioritize refactoring will cause wasted effort on phantom violations; each import contributing to this count must be individually verified before acting on it

CLIError living in src/cli/errors.ts while being consumed by domain-layer code (src/core/move.ts) is the sole verified tier inversion in the rex package. A package-level error module (src/errors.ts) is the standard pattern for shared error types used across multiple layers — its absence forced domain code to reach upward into the CLI layer

No dedicated integration test zone exists in the rex package — 5 integration tests are scattered across source-code zones by co-location gravity, making integration test coverage invisible to zone health reporting and preventing the kind of cohesion discipline that makes rex-e2e-test-suite (cohesion 1.0) measurable and enforceable

epic-correlation.ts creates a transitive dependency chain from prd-structural-validation → src/core → src/analyze/dedupe.js that bypasses zone boundary accounting — the direct zone-coupling table shows validate→core: 17 but the effective coupling reaches into the analyze subsystem; zone health metrics systematically undercount coupling when production files in one zone import from utility files in a second zone that in turn imports from a third zone

The rex package's adapter contract (src/store/contracts.ts) has no stable, independently-zoned home — it lives inside integration-adapter-layer alongside implementation files, meaning the interface and implementations share a zone boundary rather than the implementations depending on a stable interface zone; this is the classic missing ports-and-adapters layer that would give the adapter registry a fixed dependency target without coupling to Notion or Jira internals

The three algorithmic micro-zones with non-semantic IDs (unit-2, unit-3, fix) share a common characteristic: all have zone IDs that are either generic verbs or sequential numbers with zero semantic content. A zone naming policy requiring noun-phrase IDs (matching the convention of prd-domain-operations, llm-analysis-engine, etc.) would prevent future community-detection runs from registering meaningless zone names into the zone registry without a human review step

The intra-package cross-zone import graph has no enforcement mechanism parallel to the cross-package gateway tests in domain-isolation.test.js — a file in cli-mcp-interface can import directly from a file in prd-domain-operations without going through any declared gateway, and this violation will not be caught by any existing automated test; the gateway discipline that is rigorously enforced at the package boundary is structurally absent at the zone boundary within each package

src/errors.ts (the recommended home for CLIError) does not exist yet, meaning the fix for the sole verified tier inversion (core/move.ts importing CLIError from src/cli/errors.ts) requires creating a new file with no established zone membership — without a preemptive zone pin, community detection will assign the new file to whichever zone has the strongest co-location gravity, likely cli-mcp-interface, immediately recreating the instability it was meant to solve

The five zones with cohesion ≥ 1.0 or cohesion = 0 (notion-integration-client, rex-e2e-test-suite, rex-runtime-state, project-configuration, unit-store) share a structural characteristic: each is either a single-purpose directory or a pure data island with no import edges. The cohesion formula is only informative in the 0.1–0.9 range; zones at the extremes (0 or 1) are reporting boundary conditions, not health signals, and should be excluded from package-average cohesion calculations to prevent them from inflating or masking the true average

The cross-zone import table contains entries where both the source and target zone IDs carry the unit- prefix (unit-analyze → unit: 27, unit → unit-analyze: 13) — these two zones have a bidirectional 40-import relationship with misleading names that imply test scope; no documentation or enforcement rule clarifies whether unit- zones may contain production code, leaving the tier boundary between test infrastructure and production code implicit and unverifiable by zone name alone

The three micro-zones with critically low cohesion (prd-repair-command: 0.25, task-selection-engine: 0.17, schema-storage-reporting: 0.17) each contain exactly two production source files — this is below the minimum file count for reliable cohesion calculation; cohesion scores for zones with fewer than 5 production files are statistically unreliable and should be excluded from automated health gate checks to prevent false positives

No zone in the rex package has an enforced maximum size — prd-domain-operations reached 43 files with no automated trigger for review; a size ceiling of 20–25 production files per zone would force architectural review before zones reach the current concentration level

The cross-zone import table shows zero outbound imports from the e2e and unit-store zones — either test-file imports are excluded from the import graph (meaning zone coupling metrics only reflect production→production edges) or these zones genuinely make no external imports; this assumption must be documented so coupling metrics are not misinterpreted as full-graph measurements

The rex package has no pure-interface zones — every zone mixes type definitions with implementations, making it impossible to stabilize a contract between layers without coupling to the implementing zone; extracting src/store/contracts.ts into a standalone adapter-contracts zone would provide a stable dependency target for both the store implementation and its consumers

Five zones are candidates for dissolution with zero code change required (prd-repair-command, task-selection-engine, schema-storage-reporting, tree-dag-primitives, and their satellites) — dissolving all five would reduce the zone count by roughly 40% and raise average package cohesion purely through reassignment, without touching any production logic

The naming pattern across zones alternates between action-noun ('task-selection-engine', 'prd-repair-command') and domain-noun ('prd-domain-operations', 'integration-adapter-layer') without a documented convention — a standardized zone naming policy (noun-role suffix: -layer, -engine, -command, -store) would make zone roles parseable from names alone

The rex package has no 'feature zone' archetype — all zones fall into either precision islands (cohesion 1.0, ≤4 files: notion-integration-client, rex-e2e-test-suite) or aggregate zones (cohesion ≤0.44, ≥7 files: prd-domain-operations, cli-mcp-interface, etc.). The absence of healthy mid-size zones (8-15 files, cohesion 0.6-0.8) indicates the package lacks a stable feature decomposition between foundation primitives and top-level CLI dispatch.

Summing all inbound imports to prd-domain-operations: unit-cli (39) + cli (31) + unit (24) + validate (17) + store (18) + tree-dag (9) + analyze-related zones ≈ 138+ inbound imports. This fan-in count means prd-domain-operations has no safe refactoring surface — every internal restructuring requires coordinated updates across 6+ consumer zones simultaneously. The package needs a stable narrow public API layer (src/core/public.ts) that stabilizes the interface exposed to consumers before any internal core restructuring is attempted.

Every zone with coupling > 0.6 in the rex package contains at least one file from src/cli/commands/ AND at least one file from src/core/ or src/store/ — the high coupling metric is not measuring internal zone fragmentation but is instead detecting that Louvain grouped CLI and domain files into the same community. This means improving coupling scores requires file reorganization or zone pinning, not import graph cleanup.

The rex package has no 'ports' zone — adapter contracts (src/store/contracts.ts) live inside integration-adapter-layer alongside their implementations. This is structurally identical to defining an interface in the same file as its implementation: zone health tooling cannot detect when implementation files bypass the interface because interface and implementations share a zone boundary. A dedicated src/store/ports/ directory pinned to its own zone would make interface-vs-implementation separation measurable.

All five zones analyzed in this batch have either perfect health (cohesion 1.0) or critically poor health (cohesion ≤ 0.38) with nothing in between — the bimodal distribution confirms the package has two structural regimes that do not communicate via healthy intermediate abstraction layers; bridging this gap requires deliberate zone design, not just pinning or merging.

Establish a zone naming policy that requires all zone IDs to be noun phrases describing architectural responsibility (e.g. prd-domain-operations, llm-analysis-engine) and rejects single-word verbs (fix) or algorithmic sequences (unit-2, unit-3) — enforce this via a validation step in the sourcevision analyze pipeline that warns when a new zone ID does not match the noun-phrase pattern

Add an intra-package zone-boundary test (parallel to domain-isolation.test.js for cross-package imports) that enumerates declared zone memberships and asserts that files in one zone do not import directly from files in a non-adjacent zone — this would catch tier-skip violations like unit-cli → store and analyze → store/atomic-write that are currently invisible to all existing enforcement

Pre-create src/errors.ts with a zone pin to a stable foundation zone (e.g. prd-domain-operations or a new shared-errors zone) before relocating CLIError — creating the file without a pin risks community detection absorbing it into cli-mcp-interface on the next analyze run, immediately recreating the tier inversion it was meant to resolve

Package-average cohesion is skewed by zones at the boundary extremes (0 for data islands, 1.0 for single-file zones) — exclude zones with zero import edges (cohesion = 0) and single-file zones (cohesion = 1 by definition) from the package-level cohesion average to surface the true health signal of the production source zones

Exclude zones with fewer than 5 production source files from automated cohesion/coupling health gates — micro-zones produce statistically unreliable metrics that generate false warnings and distract from genuine architectural signals

Document a maximum zone size of 20–25 production files in architecture policy; prd-domain-operations at 43 files has grown past any threshold that would trigger review, and the absence of a size ceiling means there is no automated forcing function for decomposition

Standardize zone naming to a noun-role suffix pattern: -layer (infrastructure/CLI), -engine (algorithms), -command (CLI entry points), -store (persistence), -schema (types/validation) — the current mix of patterns makes zone role ambiguous when reading zone names in dependency graphs

Add a zone detection configuration rule that excludes tests/** files as community members in Louvain partitioning — test files should inherit their source file's zone assignment rather than attract source files into proximity-based micro-zones; this single algorithm change would dissolve all three test-adjacency artifact zones simultaneously

Create src/core/public.ts as a stable narrow API surface for prd-domain-operations — expose only the types and functions that CLI-tier consumers need, and redirect the 138+ inbound imports to this surface rather than to internal core files; this decouples consumer zones from core internals and enables safe core restructuring

Create a dedicated 'adapter-ports' zone pinned to src/store/ports/ containing contracts.ts and any future interface definitions — separating the port definition zone from the adapter implementation zone makes it possible for zone health tooling to detect when implementations bypass the interface

Add an architecture policy test asserting that no zone has both cohesion < 0.4 AND coupling > 0.6 simultaneously without a corresponding open PRD task — this converts the dual-threshold breach into a CI failure rather than a passive health warning, preventing new zones from silently entering the warning zone

The implicit naming convention for e2e tests (cli-{command}.test.ts) is enforced only by social convention; codify it in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md alongside the gateway pattern documentation so new contributors encounter the convention before writing their first test
